description: GEM-Adaptyv RBX-1 Binder Design Competition Ahmed Selim Üzüm1,2,*, Enes Emre Taş1,3, Abdülhamid Belen1,3, Nida Yaren Yımaz1,3, Kemal Demirtaş1,3, Türkan Haliloğlu1,3 1 Polymer Research Center, Boğaziçi University, İstanbul Türkiye 2 Department of Molecular Biology and Genetics, Boğaziçi University, İstanbul Türkiye 3 Department of Chemical Engineering, Boğaziçi University, İstanbul Türkiye * Correspondance: [email-redacted] We performed disordered protein sequence based multiple sequence alignment binding site analysis on the N termini of RBX1 to discover potential interfaces that can emerge from its disordered structure. We then used the predicted N termini binding sites in combination with the naturally occurring binding interfaces to generate binders using BoltzGen. While designing binders, only the stabilized C-terminal RING-H2 finger domain’s structure information from the unbound NMR structure of RBX1 was given to Boltz to avoid limiting the conformational diversity of the predicted N termini regions. Overall, approximately 9419 designs that passed the suggested BoltzGen filters have been generated. In addition to the recommended BoltzGen filters, the final designs were chosen based on their N termini binding interfaces, their consistency with the naturally occurring binders for the C-terminal RING-H2 finger domain and the diversity of binders.
method_summary: We then used the predicted N termini binding sites in combination with the naturally occurring binding interfaces to generate binders using BoltzGen.

description: IDR-Thunder: Predicting affinity of synthetic binders to intrinsically disordered regions Intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs) make up 5% of all residues in the PDB1, but they are poorly understood. IDRs are challenging to assess by crystallography2, and most approved drugs do not target IDRs3; as such, data needed to understand and recapitulate IDR binding using synthetic binders is disproportionately rarer than for ordered regions. Current binder design and affinity prediction tools tend to design algorithms around properties of ordered complexes. Both PRODIGY affinity estimation and BoltzGen binder design ranking train their algorithms based on datasets of primarily ordered complexes4 (i.e. ectodomains of well- characterized signaling proteins). As such, neither PRODIGY nor BoltzGen ranking metrics do a good job of predicting affinity of IDR complexes (shown below on an extended dataset of IDR complexes with affinity data based on DIBS [“DIBS+”])5. The nature of IDR binding is quite distinct from ordered, due to the sizable entropy loss during the transition from disordered monomer to bound complex. IDR interactions (IDRIs) take diverse strategies to overcome this and achieve binding. Molecular recognition features (MoRFs) are IDRs that transition from disordered to ordered (primarily helical) upon binding6. This subtype of IDRI relies on making many energetically favorable bonds to stabilize the complex. Other IDRIs form large extended hydrophobic interfaces, which directly generate a large amount of favorable enthalpy, as well as indirect favorable entropy from the release of formerly solvating water molecules. We assessed residue and structural properties of structures in DIBS+ and clustered them by K-means, identifying four primary clusters of IDRI subtypes. Cluster A likely corresponds to helical MoRFs (high helical content, many interactions per residue), B and C to hydrophobic IDRIs (middling helical content, high hydrophobicity, varying interactions...
method_summary: Current binder design and affinity prediction tools tend to design algorithms around properties of ordered complexes.

description: Method write up: For these entries a sabdab camelid nanobody was chosen essentially at random. I tried to find one that simply looked normal enough. Select amino acids on RBX1 were chosen. I chose to target either the larger alpha helix in the core, random AA’s in the undefined/unstructured tail region or, for fun, a few of the conserved cysteines in the core. Using the Old but Gold Rfantibody, rough binder backbones were created, refined with PPIflow, sequence called, refined again, and structure validated using a combo of either protenix or the venerable Boltz-2. Sorting during initial backbone generation can be summarized as a mix of target orientation screening, target contact count, target/epitope mean distance of CDR loop, and general vibes. Goofy CDR sequences got screened out manually as best able during sequence design. During refinement I implemented the recently released PPIflow, hoping to see the benefit of the maturation and refinement mechanisms but this is an ongoing work. For sorting PPIflow outputs, I mostly relied on PPIflows internal metrics, and vibes. Implausible backbones were further removed here, but I ran out of time to meaningfully screen beyond this.

description: 4F52 4P5O 1. Extract RBX1 and trim the N- terminus. Since the refolding model must fold against a fixed target structure, including this region could make prediction less reliable. We therefore excluded the N-terminus and kept only the structured RBX1 core from start 2. Hotspot Selection We selected a W87/R91-centered RBX1 hotspot because this surface has the strongest direct functional evidence for E2Ub recruitment and catalytic activation. Spratt et al. identified helix α2 residues of RBX1 as essential for CDC34ubiquitin binding and activation, with W87 and R91 emerging as key residues. We then expanded this core to include nearby hydrophobic support residues such as I44, P95, and L96, not because all are equally validated catalytic hotspots, but because a protein binder generally requires a broader and more physically grip-able footprint than a two-residue functional core. This epitope is further supported by the GLMN–RBX1–CUL1 structure, which shows that a macromolecular inhibitor can bind the RBX1 RING domain and mask the E2-binding surface. 1.Spratt DE, Wu K, Kovacev J, Pan ZQ, Shaw GS. Selective recruitment of an E2~ubiquitin complex by an E3 ubiquitin ligase. J Biol Chem. 2012. PMID 22433864. 2.Duda DM et al. Structure of a Glomulin-RBX1-CUL1 complex: inhibition of a RING E3 ligase through masking of its E2-binding surface. Mol Cell. 2012. 3.Zheng N et al. Structure of the Cul1-Rbx1-Skp1-F boxSkp2 SCF ubiquitin ligase complex. Nature. 2002. 4.Liwocha J et al. Mechanism of millisecond Lys48-linked poly-ubiquitin chain formation by cullin-RING ligases. Nat Struct Mol Biol. 2024. 5.Chen X et al. Mechanism of Ψ-Pro/C-degron recognition by the CRL2 complex. Nat Commun. 2024. Useful for broader RBX1 surface contacts including I44/W87/R91. In 2LGV, the N-terminal segment shows conformational variability, suggesting it is not a stable surface for template-based design. 2LGV

description: 1 Computational Methods for de novo Binder Design and Iterative Optimization Methods Summary: Integrated Pipeline using Boltz2 | Nanome | MARA Overview We employed an event-driven, multi-agent computational pipeline to perform de novo binder design and iterative sequence optimization against RBX1. The system integrates structure prediction via Boltz2, inversefolding sequence design (ProteinMPNN1 / SolubleMPNN2), agentic reasoning (Claude Sonnet), and Nanome's MARA platform for collaborative 3D molecular analysis. All pipeline stages run asynchronously in a shared event-bus architecture that keeps each computational step isolated, observable, and independently recoverable. Agentic Architecture The pipeline is built on a modular, event-driven multi-agent framework. A central Orchestrator acts as the coordinator and sole source of truth for task state, while specialized agents each own exactly one computational responsibility. No agent directly mutates another's state. Our pipeline can be ran using standard deterministic scripts, or we have also enabled an agent_oversight flag, which triggers an Agent run automatically after each design round. The agent loads the top-k candidate structures predicted by Boltz2 and computes Cα–Cα distances between binder and target chains (threshold: X Å defined by user) to identify which binder residues consistently form contacts across the winner ensemble. Contact frequencies are aggregated across all top-k structures, and the resulting frequency table is passed to Claude Sonnet via the Anthropic API. Claude reasons about which residues (up to 15) should be frozen for the next ProteinMPNN round, balancing the preservation of high frequency interfacial contacts against the need for continued sequence diversity. De Novo Binder Design Workflow De novo binder campaigns begin from a specified target protein structure and a user-defined region of interest (ROI), a set of target residues that the binder must engage. description: RBX1 binder design Plato March 2026 1 Design For the design task, we used our in-house protein sequence-structure co-design diffusion model to generate protein binders for RBX1. We applied both Cα- based and backbone-based variants and pooled the resulting designs. The target structure was PDB entry 1LDJ. Structure generation was initialized with noise on segments overlapping RBX1 residues 87–97, with varying levels of Gaussian perturbation. Sequence generation was initialized with random residues. We experimented with binder lengths from 10 to 40. 2 Ranking & selection We generated 10 candidates for each binder length and model variant. The generated binder backbone structures were first directly evaluated by Rosetta InterfaceAnalyzerMover. For the Cα-based model, we first heuristically ad- justed the backbone positioning. The generations were ranked primarily by interface energy and energy density, while also considering H-bond efficiency and dSASA. The backbones of the top 600 binders (100 from the Cα-based model and 500 from the backbone-based model) were then subjected to local refinement using Rosetta FastRelax. Next, side chains on both the binder and the target were optimized with PackRotamersMover, followed by a global opti- mization with MinMover. The top 30 were then selected using the same ranking criteria, with additional consideration given to whether the interaction covered the intended RBX1 87–97 segment. 1

description: Gradient-Based Binder Design with Multi-Sample Validation GEM x Adaptyv RBX1 Binder Design Competition Christian Kirkup Independent Researcher [email-redacted] 1. Overview We design protein binders for RBX1 using gradient-based sequence optimization through the Boltz-2 structure prediction model (Wohlwend et al. 2025), regularized with ProteinMPNN inverse folding (Dauparas et al. 2022). Rather than generating candidate structures with a diffusion model and filtering post-hoc, we directly optimize Position- Specific Scoring Matrices (PSSMs) to maximize Boltz-2's predicted binding confidence. This approach, implemented in the Mosaic framework (Boyd & Guns 2026), achieved 90% experimental hit rate on the Nipah G protein target. Our key methodological contribution is multi-sample validation: we re-score all candidate designs with 6 independent Boltz-2 diffusion samples and rank by median ipTM rather than single-sample scores. This filters out designs that achieve high confidence on one stochastic sample but fail to reproduce—a critical quality control step that substantially changes the ranking compared to single-sample evaluation. 2. Target Analysis RBX1 (108 aa, PDB: 2LGV) presents a dual structural challenge: an intrinsically disordered N-terminal region (residues 1–39) and a compact RING-H2 finger domain (residues 40–108) coordinating three zinc ions. We analyzed six PDB structures (2LGV, 1LDJ, 1LDK, 1U6G, 2HYE, 3DPL) showing RBX1 in complex with E2 enzymes, cullins, and regulatory proteins to identify accessible binding surfaces. Following the observation from the Nipah competition that unconstrained optimization can discover better binding surfaces than manual epitope specification (Boyd & Guns 2026), we did not specify hotspot residues. The optimizer autonomously identifies the optimal binding surface on this compact target. 3. Method Sequence representation.
